title: Ensure 'HTTP version' is set to '2.0' (if in use)

input_row.title: Ensure 'HTTP version' is set to '2.0' (if in use)
input_row.description: Periodically, newer versions are released for HTTP, either due to security flaws or to include additional functionalities. Using the latest HTTP version allows apps to take advantage of security fixes, if any, and/or new functionalities of the newer version.
input_row.rationale: Newer versions may contain security enhancements and additional functionalities. Using the latest version is recommended in order to take advantage of enhancements and new capabilities. With each software installation, organizations need to determine if a given update meets their requirements. They must also verify the compatibility and support provided for any additional software against the update revision that is selected. HTTP 2.0 has additional performance improvements for the head-of-line blocking problem of the old HTTP version, header compression, and request prioritization. HTTP 2.0 no longer supports HTTP 1.1's chunked transfer encoding mechanism, as it provides its own, more efficient mechanisms for data streaming.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. Go to App Services. 2. Click the name of an app. 3. Under Deployment, click Deployment slots. 4. Click the name of a deployment slot. 5. Under Settings, click Configuration. 6. In the General settings pane, under Platform settings, ensure that HTTP version is set to 2.0. 7. Repeat steps 1-6 for each app and deployment slot. Audit from Azure CLI Run the following command to list apps: az webapp list For each app, run the following command to list deployment slots: az webapp deployment slot list --resource-group --name For each deployment slot, run the following command to get the http20Enabled setting: az resource show --name web --resource-group -- namespace Microsoft.Web --resource-type config --parent sites//slots/ --query properties.http20Enabled Ensure that true is returned. Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: 4dcfb8b5-05cd-4090-a931-2ec29057e1fc - Name: 'App Service app slots should use latest 'HTTP Version''
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. Go to App Services. 2. Click the name of an app. 3. Under Deployment, click Deployment slots. 4. Click the name of a deployment slot. 5. Under Settings, click Configuration. 6. In the General settings pane, under Platform settings, set HTTP version to 2.0. 7. Click Save. 8. Click Continue. 9. Repeat steps 1-8 for each app and deployment slot requiring remediation. Remediate from Azure CLI For each deployment slot requiring remediation, run the following command to enable http20Enabled: az resource update --name web --resource-group -- namespace Microsoft.Web --resource-type config --parent sites//slots/ --set properties.http20Enabled=true
input_row.impact: Most modern browsers support the HTTP/2 protocol over TLS only, while non-encrypted traffic continues to use HTTP/1.1. To ensure that client browsers connect to your app with HTTP/2, either purchase an App Service Certificate for your app's custom domain or bind a third-party certificate. NOTE: HTTP/2 cannot be used in tandem with mutual authentication or client certificates.
input_row.default_value: By default, HTTP version is set to 1.1.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Navigate to the AWS Management Console:**
   - Open the AWS Management Console and log in.
   - Go to the Amazon EC2 service.

2. **Select the Load Balancers or App Service:**
   - Choose "Load Balancers" if using Elastic Load Balancing (ALB/NLB) or locate any relevant application service (like AWS App Runner or ECS).
   - For a load balancer, click on the name of the load balancer or application service.

3. **Check the Listener Configuration:**
   - Navigate to the "Listeners" tab or equivalent settings depending on the service.
   - For each listener, check the "Protocol" and "Port" settings to ensure HTTP/2.0 (where applicable) is specified as the protocol for any associated target groups or services.

4. **Verify HTTP version settings:**
   - Check if there is a specific HTTP version configuration. Look for options that indicate the use of HTTP/2 (e.g., "HTTP/2" should be mentioned in the configuration settings).
   - If HTTP version settings are located on individual application services like AWS App Runner, follow similar steps there.

5. **What to Verify/Validate:**
   - Ensure that every load balancer or relevant application service is configured to support and use HTTP/2.

6. **Expected Secure Configuration:**
   - HTTP/2 should be enabled where supported and the configurations should reflect that setting.

7. **Confirm Compliance:**
   - Document findings in relation to each load balancer or application service. If HTTP/2 is not enabled, note it as a non-compliant item and provide recommendations for remediation based on AWS best practices. 

**Note:** The audits rely heavily on navigation through interfaces and verifying settings, which may not be fully accessible via APIs or CLI, hence the manual nature of the process.
